lessons.rule: Do not use 'axios'. Use native 'node:http' or 'fetch' if available. PRD V4 strictly prohibits external HTTP clients to minimize dependencies.
Do not use 'express'. Use 'fastify' for high-performance requirements as per PRD V4.
Before generating any task, confirm that the PRD (Product Requirements Document) is fully loaded and all required sections are defined; otherwise, halt and request the missing PRD content.
Files containing JSX must use the .tsx extension and include the necessary React import (or rely on automatic JSX runtime) to avoid TypeScript syntax errors during compilation.
Always validate and sanitize file paths to prevent directory traversal; reject any path that attempts to escape the project workspace.
Never allow file paths that escape the project root; validate and sanitize all paths to prevent directory traversal.
Always validate and sanitize file paths to prevent directory traversal; reject any path containing '..' or absolute paths outside the project workspace.
Never allow file paths that escape the project root; validate and sanitize paths before writing.
Never allow file paths that escape the project root; validate and sanitize all file paths before writing.

Always validate and sanitize file paths to ensure they remain within the project workspace; reject any path that attempts to traverse outside the root directory (e.g., containing '..' or absolute paths outside the allowed workspace).
Never allow file paths that escape the project workspace; validate and sanitize all paths to prevent directory traversal attacks.
Always run TypeScript type-check (tsc --noEmit) on new or modified files to catch syntax errors before marking a task as complete.
Always run TypeScript type checking (tsc) as part of the build/CI pipeline to catch syntax errors in .ts/.tsx files early, preventing runtime failures due to invalid syntax.
Always run TypeScript compilation (tsc --noEmit) on new or modified TSX/TS files to catch syntax errors before marking a task as done.
Ensure that prompts sent to the Coder agent stay within the model's token limit to avoid empty responses; break down large tasks or trim unnecessary context.
Run TypeScript type-check (tsc --noEmit) on all .ts/.tsx files as a gate before considering a task complete; integrate this check into CI/lint to fail fast on syntax errors.

Always validate TSX/TypeScript syntax with `tsc --noEmit` (or equivalent lint) before committing; fix any syntax errors such as missing parentheses, incorrect JSX, or invalid TypeScript constructs to prevent build failures.

Always ensure the model's response stays within its token limit; if a request risks exceeding the limit (e.g., finish=length with empty stream), truncate the prompt or split the task into smaller calls to avoid empty responses.

Always ensure that generated responses stay within the model's token limit; if a task risks exceeding the limit, break it into smaller subtasks or request a continuation rather than allowing the stream to end with an empty response.
All generated files must be placed under one of the approved project root directories: apps/, packages/, or supabase/. Files outside these directories will be rejected.
Always run TypeScript type checking (tsc --noEmit) on all .ts/.tsx files and resolve any syntax errors before moving forward; treat tsc failures as blocking issues.

All generated source files must be placed within the project's allowed root directories (apps/, packages/, supabase/). Files outside these roots (e.g., under src/) violate the PATH VIOLATION rule and will be rejected.
Always keep LLM responses within the model's token limit; if a task risks exceeding the limit, break it into smaller sub‑tasks or request a continuation rather than allowing the stream to end empty.

When generating test mock files, ensure all imports are resolvable and the file is syntactically valid TypeScript (run `tsc --noEmit` on the mock) before executing the test suite to avoid syntax‑error failures.
Always run TypeScript type checking (e.g., `tsc --noEmit`) on new or modified .ts/.tsx files and resolve any syntax errors before marking a task as complete.

Always validate TypeScript syntax with `tsc --noEmit` on individual files before committing; fix any syntax errors such as missing semicolons, incorrect import/export statements, or invalid JSX to prevent compilation failures.
Keep the context provided to the Coder agent within the model's token limit to prevent empty responses due to length truncation.

Always keep the model's response within its token limit; if a response is approaching the limit, truncate it and continue in a subsequent call or request a higher max_tokens setting to avoid empty streams.
Always validate TypeScript syntax with `tsc --noEmit` (or equivalent linting) before committing changes to prevent build failures due to syntax errors.
Always keep prompts and expected responses within the model's token limit; if a request nears the limit, break the task into smaller subtasks or truncate context to avoid empty stream endings.

Ensure LLM responses stay within the model's token limit; if the response would be truncated, either request a continuation with increased max_tokens or truncate gracefully and ask for the next chunk to avoid empty outputs.
Keep the combined size of the prompt and the expected completion within the model's token limit to avoid empty stream endings; if needed, split the task into smaller chunks or reduce context.
Always validate generated mock TypeScript files with `tsc --noEmit` before executing tests to catch syntax errors early.
Always run `tsc --noEmit` (or equivalent type-check) to catch syntax errors before marking a task as done; treat any TypeScript compilation failure as a blocking error.

When delegating tasks to the Coder agent, keep the provided context minimal and well under the model's token limit (e.g., <2000 tokens) to avoid the stream ending with an empty response due to length truncation.
Always run TypeScript type-check (tsc --noEmit) on new or modified .tsx/.ts files to catch syntax errors before marking a task as done.

Ensure that LLM responses stay within the model's token limit; if a task may produce long output, break it into smaller subtasks or increase the allowed max_output_tokens to avoid empty streams due to length truncation.
Never exceed the model's maximum token limit; ensure generated responses stay within the allowed token count and handle truncation or streaming errors gracefully.
Always verify TypeScript syntax with `tsc --noEmit` before marking a task as complete; fix any syntax errors.
Always run `tsc --noEmit` to verify TypeScript syntax before considering a task complete.
Keep prompts within the model's token limit to avoid empty responses; break large tasks into smaller chunks.

Always ensure the model's response stays within the token limit; if the prompt or expected output approaches the limit, truncate, summarize, or split the request into smaller calls to avoid empty stream endings.
Ensure all files containing JSX use the .tsx extension and have valid TypeScript JSX syntax; run `tsc --noEmit` on individual files to catch syntax errors early.

Always keep the total token count of the prompt well below the model's limit (e.g., <7000 tokens for an 8192-token window) to prevent the stream from ending with an empty response. If the context is large, truncate or summarize it, or split the request into smaller chunks before sending to the Coder.
All .tsx files must contain valid JSX/TypeScript syntax; run `tsc --noEmit` as a gate to catch syntax errors before proceeding.

When compiling .tsx files with tsc, ensure tsconfig.json includes the appropriate JSX setting (e.g., "jsx": "react-jsx" or "react") and that React is imported in any file using JSX syntax. Missing JSX configuration or React import leads to syntax errors during type checking.

When delegating a task to the Coder, ensure the expected output size stays within the model's token limit (e.g., < 6000 tokens for safe completion). If a task would require a larger file or response, break it into smaller atomic sub‑tasks or request incremental generation (e.g., generate file in chunks) to avoid the stream ending with an empty response due to length cutoff.
Always ensure that LLM responses stay within the model's token limit; truncate or split outputs to prevent empty streams caused by hitting the limit.
Run `tsc --noEmit` (or equivalent type‑check) to validate syntax before marking a task as done.
Run `tsc --noEmit` to verify TypeScript syntax before marking a task as done.

Always monitor token usage; if a response approaches the model's limit (e.g., >75% of 8192 tokens), truncate the output or split the task into smaller calls to avoid empty streams due to length finish.
Always keep generated output within the model's token limit; if a response would exceed it, truncate early and request a continuation or split the task into smaller parts to avoid empty streams.
Always run TypeScript type-check (tsc --noEmit) on new or modified .ts files before marking a task as complete to catch syntax errors early.
Ensure generated responses stay within the model's token limit; if approaching the limit, truncate or split the response into multiple parts to avoid empty outputs.

Always keep the model's output within its token limit; if a response is approaching the limit, truncate it or split the request into multiple calls to avoid empty streams caused by hitting the max token threshold.

Ensure that the model's response is not truncated by setting a sufficient max_tokens limit and properly handling stream completion (e.g., checking for finish reason and not treating length cutoff as a valid response).
Supabase migration scripts must include storage bucket creation commands as part of the setup.
Healthcheck configurations must target the same port that the service actually exposes/listens on; otherwise the check will fail even if the service is healthy.

Healthcheck commands must only use utilities that are guaranteed to exist in the container image; if a required tool (e.g., nc, curl) might be missing, either install it in the Dockerfile or replace the check with a portable alternative (e.g., using /bin/true, wget, or a built‑in TCP check).

Do not configure CORS middleware with origin: true (which reflects any request origin). Instead, explicitly define a whitelist of allowed origins or use a validation function that returns only trusted origins.
Never configure CORS middleware with origin: true; always specify an explicit list of allowed origins or use a validation function that restricts origins to trusted domains.
Always run TypeScript type-check (tsc --noEmit) on all TypeScript files, including test scripts, to catch syntax errors before committing.
